---
title: "Prototype: a graded exercise"
subtitle: "Fill-in-the-blank with automatic feedback, via quarto-live"
format: live-html
engine: knitr
toc: false
webr:
  packages:
    - dplyr
live:
  show-hints: true
  show-solutions: true
---

{{< include ./_extensions/r-wasm/live/_knitr.qmd >}}
{{< include ./_extensions/r-wasm/live/_gradethis.qmd >}}

::: callout-note
## What this is (for Tim and Jamie)
This is a **proof of concept**, not part of the June 25 lesson. It shows what the
`quarto-live` extension can do that our current `quarto-webr` setup cannot: **graded
fill-in-the-blank exercises with instant feedback**, still running entirely in the browser
(no install, no server).

Try the exercise below: run it with the blanks still in (you'll get a nudge), then fill them
in and run again to see the pass feedback. The **Hint** and **Solution** buttons come from
the same system.

If we like the feel, the §7 challenges in the main lesson could become exercises like this.
The cost is converting the chunks, so this is a v2 decision, after the 25th.
:::

## Challenge: how big is the effect?

A small p-value tells us a difference is *probably real*, but not *how big* it is. Let's get
the average change in heart pumping capacity for each group of sheep.

**Fill in the two blanks:** group the data by the treatment column, then take the average of
the change.

```{webr}
#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_effect
library(dplyr)
sheep <- read.csv(
  "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jt14den/neurocamp-stats-viz/main/data/stem-cell.csv"
) |>
  mutate(change = after - before)
```

```{webr}
#| exercise: ex_effect
sheep |>
  group_by(______) |>
  summarise(avg_change = mean(______))
```

::: { .hint exercise="ex_effect" }
The column that says which group each sheep is in is `trmt`. The number you want the average
of is the one we built earlier, `change`.
:::

```{webr}
#| exercise: ex_effect
#| solution: true
sheep |>
  group_by(trmt) |>
  summarise(avg_change = mean(change))
```

```{webr}
#| exercise: ex_effect
#| check: true
gradethis::grade_this_code()
```

When you get it right, you should see the control group with a **negative** average change
(hearts got worse) and the stem-cell group with a **positive** one (hearts improved).
